meritorious persons, which in turn leads to lowering of 
efficiency of administration. The submission, therefore, 
is that reservation should be confined to a small minority 
of appointments/posts, — in any event, to not more than 
30%, the figure referred to in the speech of Dr Ambedkar 
in the Constituent Assembly. The mandate of Article 335, it 
is argued, implies that reservations should be so operated 
as not to affect the efficiency of administration. Even Article 
16 and the directive of Article 46, it is said, should be read 
subject to the aforesaid mandate of Article 335.
835. The respondents, on the other hand, contend that 
the marks obtained at the examination/test/interview 
at the stage of entry into service is not an indicium of 
the inherent merit of a candidate. They rely upon the 
opinion of Douglas, J in DeFunis [40 L Ed 2d 164 : 416 
US 312 (1974)] where the learned Judge illustrates the
